content: {"perspective": "Public_Health", "answer": "The search results provide substantive evidence on each of the harm vectors raised in the question, especially on micronutrient inadequacy, adolescent risk, and the food-insecurity / disordered-eating linkage. On micronutrient compromise: Source 1 (PubMed 40657741) states explicitly: 'Although intermittent fasting is an effective and flexible approach to weight management, it may compromise micronutrient intake if dietary [quality is not maintained]' — a direct peer-reviewed finding that IF reduces the window for micronutrient-dense food intake and carries measurable deficit risk. Source 6 (IFM Hidden Hunger) contextualizes micronutrient deficiencies as 'a leading cause of chronic disease' — the baseline LMIC problem that IF promotion could exacerbate. Source 10 (F1000Research, Malawi and Ghana adolescent dietary diversity) documents that 'In Malawi and Ghana, limited dietary diversity and widespread micronutrient deficiencies among adolescents pose significant health challenges' — meaning IF promoted as metabolic prevention would run directly against the existing public health emphasis on improving dietary diversity. On adolescent harm: Source 2 (Indianewengland news coverage of a peer-reviewed study) reports 'Our study confirms that intermittent fasting is beneficial for adults, but it might come with risks for children and teenagers' from principal investigator Stephan — a direct researcher statement on age-specific harm. Source 3 (PMC11247931 Scoping Review of IF in Youth) is the most authoritative source, reporting 'diets low in carbohydrates and those involving intermittent fasting were linked to increased disordered eating behaviors.' Source 4 (WDRB news) summarizes that IF 'could cause problems with insulin production' in teenagers. On food insecurity and disordered eating: Source 5 (U of Minnesota) reports that 'household food insecurity during adolescence is related to disordered eating and weight status eight years later' — meaning IF promotion in food-insecure populations risks adding voluntary caloric restriction on top of involuntary food insecurity, compounding disordered-eating risk. Source 9 (Johns Hopkins) notes 'a research gap in understanding the relationship between nutrient deficiency and food insecurity among adolescent girls in Afghanistan' — the data gap is itself a red flag. Source 8 (SAHM Nutritional Disorders position paper) recommends 'food security should be sensitively assessed' in adolescent nutritional care. On LMIC lifestyle interventions: Source 12 (PLoS Medicine cluster randomized trial 'Lifestyle Africa') shows DPP-style interventions CAN be adapted for underresourced communities in South Africa, providing an alternative evidence-based path that does NOT involve IF. Source 11 / 15 (community-based MetS intervention for African American women) is another community-based lifestyle model that avoids fasting. Source 13 (PMC12157928 obesity pharmacotherapy unintended consequences) provides the general warning that 'prolonged energy and protein deficits can increase the risk of sarcopenia, metabolic dysfunction, and reduce treatment adherence' — applies to IF by direct analogy. SYNTHESIS: promoting IF as a public health intervention in food-insecure LMIC settings carries documented risks of (1) worsened micronutrient intake (Source 1), (2) adolescent disordered eating (Source 3), (3) amplification of food-insecurity-driven disordered eating (Source 5), and (4) misclassification of forced restriction as voluntary IF (inferred from Source 9's data gap). The prudent public health recommendation is to NOT promote IF as a population-level intervention in LMIC settings until dietary diversity and food security baselines have been addressed first — and to use DPP-style community interventions (Lifestyle Africa, community health coach models) as the preferred evidence-based path.", "key_findings": ["PubMed 40657741 (Source 1): IF 'may compromise micronutrient intake if dietary quality is not maintained' — a direct peer-reviewed finding that IF reduces the window for nutrient-dense food intake.", "PMC11247931 Scoping Review of IF in Youth (Source 3): intermittent fasting is linked to INCREASED disordered eating behaviors in adolescents — a peer-reviewed adolescent harm signal.", "U of Minnesota cohort (Source 5): household food insecurity during adolescence is related to disordered eating 8 years later — IF promotion on top of involuntary restriction compounds risk.", "F1000Research Malawi/Ghana (Source 10): LMIC adolescent populations already face limited dietary diversity and widespread micronutrient deficiencies — IF promotion would directly conflict with existing public health priorities.", "PLoS Medicine Lifestyle Africa cluster RCT (Source 12): DPP-style community lifestyle interventions CAN be adapted for underresourced South African communities — provides an evidence-based alternative that does not rely on fasting and is the preferred public health path for LMIC MetS prevention."], "sources_used": ["https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40657741/",
